nodes.title: Handle quick primitive checks first
nodes.why: Early exits avoid unnecessary recursion for common cases like equal primitives and `NaN`.
nodes.alternative: Skip early checks and recurse through all values uniformly.
nodes.tradeoff: Much faster and clearer for common inputs, but adds a few upfront branches.

nodes.title: Compare Date and RegExp with type-specific rules

anchor.snippet: if (a instanceof Date && b instanceof Date)

nodes.why: Dates and regexps need custom comparisons; plain object recursion is not enough.
nodes.alternative: Treat them like generic objects and compare keys only.
nodes.tradeoff: Correct behavior for these types, at the cost of more branching logic.

nodes.title: Recurse through arrays and object keys
nodes.why: Deep equality is about structure, so we compare lengths/keys and recurse value-by-value.
nodes.tradeoff: More code, but reliable for richer data compared to stringify shortcuts.

nodes.title: Use JSON stringify as a quick comparison
nodes.why: It is very short and works for many plain JSON-like objects.
nodes.alternative: Implement full recursive comparison with type checks.
nodes.tradeoff: Easy to write, but misses important edge cases like `NaN`, Date, and RegExp behavior.

nodes.title: Catch errors from cyclic structures
nodes.why: `JSON.stringify` throws on cycles, so catch keeps the function from crashing.
nodes.alternative: Pre-scan objects for cycles before stringifying.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple safety net, but still does not make stringify a full deep-equality solution.

nodes.title: Return false on unsupported cases
nodes.why: When serialization fails, safest fallback is to treat inputs as not reliably equal.
nodes.alternative: Fallback to recursive deep compare after catch.
nodes.tradeoff: Keeps this approach tiny, but can reject cases a full implementation would handle.
